title: What Is a Menstrual Cycle?
body:
A menstrual cycle is the monthly series of changes your body goes through to prepare for pregnancy. Each cycle begins on the first day of your period and ends the day before your next period starts. A typical cycle lasts between 21 and 35 days.

The cycle is driven by hormones, mainly estrogen and progesterone, produced by the ovaries. These hormones cause the lining of the uterus to build up and, if pregnancy does not occur, to shed. That shedding is your period.

Every person's cycle is a little different. Tracking yours over time helps you learn what is normal for you and notice changes early.

title: The Menstrual Phase
body:
The menstrual phase is the start of your cycle. It begins on the first day of bleeding and typically lasts 3 to 7 days.

During this phase, the uterus sheds the lining it built up during the previous cycle. Hormone levels, especially estrogen and progesterone, are at their lowest, which is why many people feel tired or experience cramps.

**What you may notice:**
- Bleeding that ranges from light spotting to heavy flow
- Cramping in the lower abdomen or back
- Fatigue or low energy
- Mood changes

Flow intensity can vary from day to day and from cycle to cycle. Tracking your flow helps you understand what is typical for you.

title: The Follicular Phase
body:
The follicular phase overlaps with and extends beyond your period. It starts on the first day of menstruation and ends when ovulation begins.

During this phase, the pituitary gland releases follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), which signals the ovaries to prepare several follicles. Usually one follicle becomes dominant and continues to mature. At the same time, rising estrogen levels cause the uterine lining to thicken.

**What you may notice:**
- Gradually increasing energy after your period ends
- Improved mood as estrogen rises
- Lighter or no bleeding as menstruation wraps up

This phase typically lasts about 14 days, but it can be shorter or longer, which is the main reason cycle lengths vary between people.
sourceName: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development

title: Ovulation: What Happens and When
body:
Ovulation is the release of a mature egg from one of the ovaries. It typically happens around the middle of your cycle — roughly day 14 in a 28-day cycle — but the exact timing varies.

A surge in luteinizing hormone (LH) triggers the ovary to release the egg, which then travels down the fallopian tube. The egg can be fertilized for about 12 to 24 hours after release.

**What you may notice:**
- A slight increase in basal body temperature
- Changes in cervical mucus (clearer, more slippery)
- Mild pain on one side of the lower abdomen (sometimes called mittelschmerz)

Ovulation marks the transition from the follicular phase to the luteal phase.
sourceName: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development

title: The Luteal Phase
body:
The luteal phase is the second half of your cycle, lasting from ovulation until the start of your next period — typically about 14 days.

After the egg is released, the empty follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, which produces progesterone. Progesterone keeps the uterine lining thick and ready for a fertilized egg. If pregnancy does not occur, the corpus luteum breaks down, progesterone drops, and the lining sheds as your period.

**What you may notice:**
- Premenstrual symptoms such as bloating, breast tenderness, or mood changes
- Changes in appetite or food cravings
- Fatigue or lower energy in the days before your period

These symptoms are driven by shifting hormone levels and are a normal part of the cycle.

title: Reproductive Anatomy Basics
body:
Understanding your reproductive anatomy helps you make sense of the changes you track each cycle. The key structures work together every month to prepare for a possible pregnancy.

**The main organs:**
- **Ovaries:** Two small, almond-shaped glands that store eggs and produce estrogen and progesterone. Each cycle, one ovary typically releases a single mature egg
- **Fallopian tubes:** Two narrow tubes that connect the ovaries to the uterus. After ovulation, the egg travels through a fallopian tube, where fertilization can occur
- **Uterus:** A muscular, pear-shaped organ where a fertilized egg implants and a pregnancy develops. The inner lining (endometrium) thickens each cycle and sheds as your period if pregnancy does not occur
- **Cervix:** The lower, narrow end of the uterus that opens into the vagina. It produces mucus that changes in texture throughout the cycle
- **Vagina:** The canal that connects the cervix to the outside of the body. Menstrual blood exits through the vagina

These organs are regulated by hormones from the pituitary gland in the brain, creating the feedback loop that drives your menstrual cycle.

title: Hormones and Your Cycle
body:
Four key hormones orchestrate your menstrual cycle. Understanding their roles explains why you feel different at various points in your cycle.

**The main hormones:**
- **Estrogen:** Produced mainly by the ovaries, estrogen rises during the follicular phase. It thickens the uterine lining, boosts energy, and generally supports positive mood. Estrogen peaks just before ovulation
- **Progesterone:** Released by the corpus luteum after ovulation, progesterone stabilizes the uterine lining for possible implantation. It has a calming effect but can cause fatigue, bloating, and breast tenderness. It drops sharply before your period
- **Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH):** Produced by the pituitary gland, FSH signals the ovaries to develop follicles at the start of each cycle
- **Luteinizing hormone (LH):** Also from the pituitary gland, a surge in LH triggers ovulation, the release of a mature egg

**Why this matters for tracking:**
Many of the symptoms you log - mood shifts, energy changes, cramps, skin changes - are direct results of these hormonal fluctuations. Tracking patterns over multiple cycles can reveal your personal hormonal rhythm.
sourceName: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development

title: Cervical Mucus Changes
body:
Cervical mucus changes throughout your cycle in response to shifting hormone levels. These changes are a natural part of reproductive function and can help you understand where you are in your cycle.

**Typical patterns:**
- **During menstruation:** Mucus is not usually noticeable because of menstrual flow
- **After your period (early follicular):** Little to no mucus; you may feel relatively dry
- **Mid-follicular phase:** Mucus increases and may appear white or creamy as estrogen rises
- **Approaching ovulation:** Mucus becomes clear, slippery, and stretchy, often compared to raw egg whites. This is the most fertile-type mucus and signals that ovulation is near
- **After ovulation (luteal phase):** Progesterone causes mucus to become thicker, stickier, and less abundant

**Why tracking mucus matters:**
Cervical mucus is one of the body's observable fertility signs. Changes in its pattern can also signal hormonal shifts or health changes worth discussing with a provider.

Note that hormonal birth control, medications, and infections can alter cervical mucus patterns.
sourceName: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development

title: Pain and Cramps
body:
Menstrual cramps (dysmenorrhea) are one of the most common period symptoms. They are caused by prostaglandins — chemicals that make the uterus contract to shed its lining.

Cramps usually begin just before or at the start of your period and may last 1 to 3 days. They are most often felt in the lower abdomen but can also radiate to the lower back or thighs.

**Tips for managing cramps:**
- Over-the-counter pain relievers (such as ibuprofen) can reduce prostaglandin production
- A heating pad on the lower abdomen or back may ease discomfort
- Gentle exercise, like walking or stretching, can help relax muscles
- Staying hydrated supports overall comfort

If cramps are severe enough to interfere with daily activities or do not improve with self-care, talk to a healthcare provider.

title: Mood Changes and Your Cycle
body:
Hormonal shifts throughout the menstrual cycle can affect mood and emotional well-being. Many people notice changes in the days leading up to their period, when progesterone drops and estrogen fluctuates.

**Common mood-related experiences:**
- Irritability or feeling easily frustrated
- Sadness or tearfulness
- Anxiety or feeling on edge
- Difficulty concentrating

These changes are part of premenstrual syndrome (PMS) and are experienced by up to 3 in 4 menstruating people. For most, symptoms are mild and manageable.

**Strategies that may help:**
- Regular physical activity
- Adequate sleep
- Reducing caffeine and alcohol intake
- Stress-management techniques like deep breathing

If mood changes are severe or significantly impact your daily life, a healthcare provider can discuss additional options.

title: Digestive Symptoms
body:
Digestive changes around your period are common and are largely driven by prostaglandins — the same chemicals that cause uterine cramps. When prostaglandins affect nearby intestinal tissue, they can speed up or slow down digestion.

**What you may notice:**
- Bloating, especially in the days before your period
- Looser stools or diarrhea during the first days of bleeding
- Constipation in the luteal phase (due to progesterone slowing digestion)
- Nausea
- Changes in appetite

**Tips for comfort:**
- Eat smaller, more frequent meals
- Stay hydrated
- Include fiber-rich foods to support regularity
- Limit salty foods that can worsen bloating

Digestive symptoms that are persistent or unusually severe should be discussed with a healthcare provider.
sourceName: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development

title: Skin Changes During Your Cycle
body:
Hormonal fluctuations throughout the menstrual cycle can visibly affect your skin. Many people notice these changes in predictable patterns each month.

**What you may notice:**
- Breakouts or acne, often in the days before your period when progesterone peaks and estrogen drops
- Oilier skin in the luteal phase due to increased sebum production
- Drier skin during menstruation when estrogen is at its lowest
- A "glow" or clearer complexion around ovulation when estrogen peaks

**Tips for skin care:**
- Keep a consistent cleansing routine throughout your cycle
- Use a gentle, non-comedogenic moisturizer
- Avoid picking at breakouts, which can lead to scarring
- Consider tracking skin changes alongside your cycle to identify patterns

Persistent or severe acne that does not respond to basic skin care may benefit from guidance by a healthcare provider.

title: Fatigue and Energy
body:
Energy levels naturally rise and fall with the menstrual cycle. Understanding these patterns can help you plan your activities and practice self-care.

**Typical energy patterns:**
- **Menstrual phase:** Energy tends to be lowest, especially if bleeding is heavy
- **Follicular phase:** Rising estrogen often brings increasing energy and motivation
- **Ovulation:** Many people feel their most energetic around ovulation
- **Luteal phase:** Progesterone can cause fatigue, especially in the days before your period

**Tips for managing fatigue:**
- Prioritize sleep, especially in low-energy phases
- Stay physically active — even light movement can boost energy
- Eat balanced meals with iron-rich foods during your period
- Stay hydrated throughout your cycle

Persistent fatigue that does not follow a cyclical pattern or that significantly impacts your daily life should be discussed with a healthcare provider.

title: Libido and Your Cycle
body:
Sexual desire naturally fluctuates throughout the menstrual cycle, driven by the same hormones that regulate ovulation and menstruation. These changes are normal and vary from person to person.

**Typical patterns:**
- **Follicular phase:** As estrogen rises, many people notice a gradual increase in libido
- **Around ovulation:** Libido often peaks when estrogen is highest and testosterone (produced in small amounts by the ovaries) also surges. This is the body's natural response during the fertile window
- **Luteal phase:** Progesterone rises after ovulation and can dampen desire. Some people feel more inward-focused or less interested in sex
- **Premenstrual and menstrual days:** Libido varies widely. Some people experience increased desire due to pelvic blood flow, while others feel decreased interest due to cramps or fatigue

**Factors beyond hormones:**
- Stress, sleep quality, and relationship dynamics also play a role
- Certain medications, including hormonal birth control, can affect libido
- Tracking libido alongside your cycle can reveal your personal pattern

Significant or persistent changes in sexual desire that concern you are worth discussing with a healthcare provider.

title: Sleep and Your Cycle
body:
Hormonal changes across the menstrual cycle can affect sleep quality and duration. Many people notice predictable shifts in how well they sleep at different points in their cycle.

**How your cycle affects sleep:**
- **Follicular phase:** Rising estrogen tends to support better sleep quality and more stable energy during the day
- **Around ovulation:** Body temperature rises slightly after ovulation, which can make falling asleep a little harder for some people
- **Luteal phase:** Progesterone has a mild sedative effect and may cause daytime drowsiness, yet many people report more disrupted nighttime sleep. Premenstrual symptoms like bloating, breast tenderness, and anxiety can also interfere with rest
- **Menstrual phase:** Cramps, heavy bleeding, and hormonal lows can disturb sleep, especially on the first few nights

**Tips for better cycle-related sleep:**
- Keep a consistent sleep schedule throughout your cycle
- Create a cool, dark sleep environment, especially helpful in the luteal phase when body temperature is higher
- Limit caffeine in the afternoon, particularly during sensitive phases
- Gentle stretching or relaxation exercises before bed can ease premenstrual tension

Persistent sleep problems that do not follow a cyclical pattern may have other causes and are worth discussing with a healthcare provider.

title: Why Track Your Cycle?
body:
Tracking your menstrual cycle gives you a clearer picture of your overall health. Your cycle is considered a vital sign — changes in its length, regularity, or symptoms can provide early clues about your well-being.

**Benefits of cycle tracking:**
- **Know your patterns:** Understand what is normal for you, including cycle length, flow, and symptoms
- **Spot changes early:** Irregular cycles, missed periods, or new symptoms are easier to notice when you have a baseline
- **Plan ahead:** Anticipate when your period will arrive and prepare for high- or low-energy days
- **Inform your healthcare provider:** Detailed cycle data makes medical conversations more productive
- **Understand mood and energy:** Many people find that tracking mood and energy alongside their cycle reveals helpful patterns

Consistency is key — even logging a few details each day builds a valuable picture over time.

title: Hydration and Nutrition
body:
What you eat and drink can influence how you feel throughout your cycle. While no single diet eliminates period symptoms, balanced nutrition supports your body during each phase.

**Hydration:**
- Aim for about 8 cups (64 oz) of water per day, adjusting for activity and climate
- During your period, extra fluid loss from bleeding means hydration is especially important
- Warm beverages like herbal tea may help soothe cramps

**Nutrition tips by phase:**
- **Menstrual phase:** Iron-rich foods (leafy greens, beans, fortified cereals) help replace iron lost through bleeding
- **Follicular and ovulation phases:** Complex carbohydrates and lean proteins support rising energy
- **Luteal phase:** Magnesium-rich foods (nuts, seeds, dark chocolate) may help with bloating and mood

**General tips:**
- Limit caffeine and alcohol, which can worsen cramps and mood swings
- Reduce salty foods to help manage bloating
- Eat regular, balanced meals to maintain stable energy

title: Exercise and Your Cycle
body:
Physical activity is one of the most effective ways to manage period symptoms, and you do not need to stop exercising during your period.

**How exercise helps:**
- Releases endorphins, which are natural pain relievers and mood boosters
- Improves blood circulation, which can ease cramps
- Reduces bloating and water retention
- Supports better sleep

**Adapting to your cycle:**
- **Menstrual phase:** Gentle activities like walking, yoga, or swimming can feel good without overexertion
- **Follicular phase:** Rising energy makes this a good time for more intense workouts
- **Ovulation:** Many people feel strongest and most coordinated around ovulation
- **Luteal phase:** Moderate exercise is still beneficial; listen to your body and reduce intensity if needed

The best exercise is the kind you enjoy and can do consistently. There is no need to push through severe discomfort — rest is also an important part of wellness.

title: Mood, Hormones, and the Cycle
body:
Hormones play a significant role in how you feel emotionally throughout your cycle. Understanding this connection can help you respond to mood shifts with self-compassion rather than frustration.

**The hormone–mood connection:**
- **Estrogen** generally supports positive mood. When it rises (follicular phase), many people feel more upbeat and social
- **Progesterone** has a calming effect but can also contribute to fatigue and low mood, especially as it drops in the late luteal phase
- The **premenstrual drop** in both hormones is what triggers common PMS mood symptoms

**What may help:**
- Track your mood alongside your cycle to identify your personal patterns
- Plan demanding tasks for phases when you typically feel your best
- Practice self-care during your luteal phase: extra sleep, gentle exercise, and stress reduction
- Social connection and talking about how you feel can also make a difference

If mood changes are severe, persistent, or interfere with relationships and daily life, consider speaking with a healthcare provider about premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD).

title: Hydration and Your Menstrual Cycle
body:
Staying well-hydrated is important throughout your cycle, but your body's fluid needs shift with hormonal changes. Understanding these shifts can help you adjust your water intake.

**Why hydration matters during your period:**
- Blood loss during menstruation means your body is losing fluid that needs to be replaced
- Dehydration can worsen common menstrual symptoms like headaches, fatigue, and cramps
- Prostaglandins, the chemicals that cause uterine contractions, may trigger more discomfort when you are dehydrated

**Hydration across the cycle:**
- **Menstrual phase:** Prioritize extra fluids to offset blood loss. Warm beverages like herbal tea may also help soothe cramps
- **Follicular phase:** Standard hydration needs; rising estrogen supports fluid balance
- **Ovulation:** Some people retain slightly more water around ovulation due to hormonal shifts
- **Luteal phase:** Progesterone can promote water retention and bloating. Counterintuitively, drinking more water, not less, helps reduce bloating by signaling your body to release excess fluid

**Practical tips:**
- Aim for about 8 cups (64 oz) of water per day as a baseline, adjusting for activity, climate, and body size
- Carry a water bottle as a visual reminder
- Fruits and vegetables with high water content (cucumbers, watermelon, oranges) contribute to hydration

title: Hydration for Reproductive Health
body:
Beyond managing period symptoms, adequate hydration plays a broader role in reproductive health. Water supports many of the body's processes that are essential for a healthy menstrual cycle.

**How hydration supports reproductive function:**
- **Cervical mucus production:** Adequate fluid intake supports the production of cervical mucus, which changes throughout the cycle and plays a role in fertility
- **Blood volume:** Your body needs sufficient fluid to maintain healthy blood volume, which is especially important during menstruation when blood is being lost
- **Nutrient transport:** Water helps carry nutrients, including iron which is depleted during periods, to cells throughout the body
- **Hormone transport:** Hormones travel through the bloodstream; proper hydration supports efficient circulation
- **Temperature regulation:** The slight body temperature increase after ovulation is easier for the body to manage with good hydration

**Signs of dehydration to watch for:**
- Dark yellow urine (aim for pale yellow)
- Headaches, especially around your period
- Fatigue that seems disproportionate to your activity level
- Dizziness or lightheadedness
- Dry mouth or skin

If you notice that you consistently feel worse during certain cycle phases, try increasing your water intake during those times and see if symptoms improve.

title: Medication and Your Cycle
body:
Many common medications can interact with or be affected by your menstrual cycle. Being aware of these interactions helps you manage your health more effectively.

**Pain relievers and your period:**
- **NSAIDs** (ibuprofen, naproxen) reduce prostaglandin production, which can lessen cramps and may slightly reduce menstrual flow. They work best when taken at the first sign of pain or bleeding
- **Acetaminophen** (paracetamol) can help with pain but does not reduce inflammation or flow
- Taking pain medication on an empty stomach may cause digestive discomfort. Pair with a small snack

**How your cycle affects medication:**
- Hormone fluctuations can influence how your body absorbs and metabolizes certain drugs
- Some people notice that the same dose of a medication feels more or less effective at different points in their cycle
- The luteal phase may alter how the body processes some medications due to progesterone's effects on metabolism

**Tips for tracking medications:**
- Log medications alongside your cycle to spot patterns in effectiveness or side effects
- Note the timing of doses relative to your cycle phase
- Discuss any cycle-related medication concerns with your healthcare provider or pharmacist

Never adjust prescription medication doses without consulting your healthcare provider.

title: Common Medications and Period Effects
body:
Several categories of medication can affect your menstrual cycle. Knowing what to expect can help you track changes and have informed conversations with your healthcare provider.

**Medications that may affect your period:**
- **Hormonal birth control** (pills, patches, IUDs, injections): Designed to alter your cycle. May lighten periods, reduce cramps, or stop periods entirely depending on the method
- **Blood thinners** (anticoagulants): Can make periods heavier or longer
- **Antidepressants:** Some SSRIs and SNRIs may cause irregular periods or changes in flow
- **Thyroid medications:** Untreated thyroid problems cause irregular periods; medication that corrects thyroid levels often restores regularity
- **Steroids and anti-inflammatory drugs:** Long-term use may affect cycle regularity
- **Chemotherapy drugs:** Often cause temporary or permanent changes to menstruation

**What to track:**
- Any new medication and when you started it
- Changes in cycle length, flow, or symptoms after starting a medication
- Whether your period returns to its previous pattern after stopping a medication

**Important reminders:**
- Do not stop taking prescribed medication because of period changes without consulting your provider
- Report significant changes in your cycle when starting any new medication
- Bring your cycle tracking data to medical appointments for more productive conversations.

title: Understanding PMS and PMDD
body:
Premenstrual syndrome (PMS) and premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) are conditions linked to the hormonal changes in the luteal phase, the time between ovulation and the start of your period.

**Premenstrual syndrome (PMS):**
Up to 3 in 4 menstruating people experience some PMS symptoms. Common signs include bloating, breast tenderness, mood swings, irritability, food cravings, and fatigue. Symptoms typically appear 1 to 2 weeks before your period and resolve within a few days of bleeding starting.

**Premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD):**
PMDD is a more severe form affecting about 3 to 8 percent of menstruating people. Symptoms are similar to PMS but significantly more intense, particularly emotional symptoms like severe depression, anxiety, or anger that interfere with daily life and relationships.

**Managing PMS:**
- Regular exercise, adequate sleep, and stress management
- Balanced nutrition with reduced salt, caffeine, and alcohol
- Calcium and magnesium supplements may help some people
- Tracking symptoms helps identify personal patterns and triggers

**When to seek help:**
If premenstrual symptoms interfere with work, relationships, or daily activities, talk to a healthcare provider. PMDD is a recognized medical condition with effective treatments including medication and therapy.

title: Hormonal Birth Control and Your Cycle
body:
Hormonal birth control works by changing the natural hormone patterns that drive your menstrual cycle. Understanding these effects helps you interpret your cycle tracking data while using contraception.

**How hormonal birth control affects your cycle:**
- **Combined pills, patches, and rings:** Provide steady doses of estrogen and progestin that suppress ovulation. The "period" during the placebo week is actually withdrawal bleeding, not a true menstrual period
- **Progestin-only pills (mini-pills):** Thicken cervical mucus and thin the uterine lining. Ovulation may or may not be suppressed. Periods may become irregular or lighter
- **Hormonal IUDs:** Release progestin locally, thinning the uterine lining. Many users experience much lighter periods or no bleeding at all over time
- **Injections (e.g., Depo-Provera):** Suppress ovulation and thin the uterine lining. Periods often become irregular and may stop entirely
- **Implants:** Suppress ovulation in most users. Bleeding patterns vary widely

**Tracking while on birth control:**
- Cycle tracking is still valuable for monitoring mood, energy, symptoms, and bleeding patterns
- Changes in your bleeding pattern while on birth control can be worth noting and discussing with your provider
- After stopping hormonal birth control, it may take several cycles for your natural pattern to return

title: Cycle Tracking Through Life Stages
body:
Your menstrual cycle changes throughout your life, and tracking at every stage provides valuable health information.

**Adolescence (first years of menstruation):**
Periods are often irregular for the first 1 to 3 years as the hormonal system matures. Cycles may be longer or shorter than average, and flow can be unpredictable. Tracking helps young people learn their emerging patterns.

**Reproductive years:**
Cycles typically become more regular and predictable. This is when tracking is most useful for understanding personal patterns, managing symptoms, and identifying changes. Regular cycles are considered a vital sign of overall health.

**Pregnancy and postpartum:**
Menstruation stops during pregnancy. After delivery, the return of periods varies widely. Breastfeeding can delay resumption for months. When periods return, the pattern may differ from pre-pregnancy cycles.

**Perimenopause (typically mid-40s to early 50s):**
Hormone levels begin to fluctuate unpredictably. Cycles may become shorter, longer, heavier, lighter, or more irregular. Tracking during this transition helps distinguish normal changes from those worth discussing with a provider.

**Menopause:**
Defined as 12 consecutive months without a period, menopause marks the end of menstrual cycles. Any bleeding after menopause should be reported to a healthcare provider.

At every stage, your cycle data tells a story about your health.
sourceName: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development

title: Heavy Flow and Large Clots
body:
Heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia) is one of the most common reasons people seek help for period problems. While flow varies from person to person, certain signs suggest your bleeding may be heavier than typical.

**Signs of heavy flow:**
- Soaking through a pad or tampon every hour for several hours
- Needing to change protection during the night
- Passing blood clots larger than a quarter (about 2.5 cm)
- Bleeding that lasts longer than 7 days
- Feeling tired, short of breath, or lightheaded (possible signs of anemia)

**Why it matters:**
Heavy bleeding can lead to iron-deficiency anemia and significantly affect quality of life. There are many possible causes, including hormonal imbalances, uterine fibroids, or clotting disorders.

**What to do:**
If you regularly experience any of the signs above, schedule an appointment with a healthcare provider. Tracking your flow intensity and clot size can provide helpful information for your visit.
sourceName: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development

title: Period Color Changes
body:
Period blood can range in color from bright red to dark brown or even black, and most color variations are completely normal. The color often reflects how quickly blood is leaving the body.

**What different colors may mean:**
- **Bright red:** Fresh blood, typically seen during the heaviest days of flow
- **Dark red:** Blood that has had time to oxidize; common at the start or end of a period
- **Brown or black:** Older blood that took longer to leave the uterus; often seen at the beginning or end of a period
- **Pink:** Blood mixed with cervical fluid; may appear during light flow or spotting

**When to talk to a provider:**
- Orange or grey-tinged discharge, which could indicate an infection
- Consistently unusual colors that are new for you
- Color changes accompanied by a strong or unusual odor
- Any bleeding that occurs between periods or after menopause

Tracking your period color over time can help you recognize what is typical for you and notice meaningful changes.

title: Irregular and Absent Periods
body:
A "regular" cycle is one that falls within a consistent range for you, typically between 21 and 35 days. Some variation is normal, but significant changes may be worth discussing with a healthcare provider.

**Types of irregularity:**
- **Oligomenorrhea:** Infrequent periods (cycles longer than 35 days)
- **Polymenorrhea:** Frequent periods (cycles shorter than 21 days)
- **Amenorrhea:** Absence of periods for 3 or more months (when not pregnant, breastfeeding, or in menopause)
- **Metrorrhagia:** Bleeding between periods

**Possible causes:**
- Stress, significant weight changes, or excessive exercise
- Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)
- Thyroid disorders
- Perimenopause
- Certain medications

**When to seek care:**
- Your period stops for 3 or more months
- Your cycle suddenly changes significantly
- You consistently experience cycles shorter than 21 days or longer than 35 days
- You bleed between periods

Keeping a record of your cycle dates makes it easier for a provider to evaluate your pattern.
sourceName: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development

title: Severe Pain
body:
While some discomfort during your period is common, severe pain that disrupts daily activities is not something you should simply accept. Pain that does not improve with over-the-counter remedies deserves medical attention.

**Signs that pain may need evaluation:**
- Cramps that are significantly worse than usual or that worsen over time
- Pain that does not respond to ibuprofen or other over-the-counter pain relief
- Pain that occurs outside of your period
- Pain during sexual intercourse
- Pain with urination or bowel movements during your period

**Possible causes of severe menstrual pain:**
- Endometriosis (tissue similar to the uterine lining grows outside the uterus)
- Adenomyosis (uterine lining grows into the muscular wall of the uterus)
- Uterine fibroids
- Pelvic inflammatory disease

**What to do:**
Track when your pain occurs, how intense it is, and what makes it better or worse. This information helps a healthcare provider determine the cause and recommend appropriate treatment. You deserve to have your pain taken seriously.

title: Pregnancy and Your Cycle
body:
Understanding the relationship between your menstrual cycle and pregnancy can help you recognize important signs and make informed health decisions.

**How pregnancy relates to your cycle:**
- Pregnancy begins when a fertilized egg implants in the uterine lining, which prevents the lining from shedding, so your period stops
- A missed period is often the first sign of pregnancy, which is one reason tracking your cycle is so valuable
- Some people experience light spotting around the time of implantation (about 6 to 12 days after ovulation), which can be mistaken for a light period

**Early signs of pregnancy:**
- A missed or unusually light period
- Breast tenderness or swelling
- Nausea, with or without vomiting
- Fatigue
- Frequent urination
- These symptoms overlap with premenstrual symptoms, which is why a pregnancy test is the most reliable way to confirm

**When to see a healthcare provider:**
- If you suspect you may be pregnant
- If you experience bleeding during a confirmed pregnancy
- If you have been trying to conceive for 12 months (or 6 months if over 35) without success
- If you have irregular cycles that make it difficult to predict ovulation

Early prenatal care leads to the best outcomes for both parent and baby.

title: Fertility Awareness
body:
Fertility awareness means understanding when during your cycle you are most likely to conceive. Whether you are trying to become pregnant or simply want to understand your body better, this knowledge is empowering.

**The fertile window:**
- You can become pregnant during a roughly 6-day window each cycle: the 5 days before ovulation and the day of ovulation itself
- Sperm can survive in the reproductive tract for up to 5 days, while the egg is viable for 12 to 24 hours after release
- In a typical 28-day cycle, this window falls around days 10 to 16, but the exact timing varies between individuals and between cycles

**Signs that may indicate ovulation:**
- Clear, stretchy cervical mucus (resembling raw egg whites)
- A slight rise in basal body temperature after ovulation
- Mild lower abdominal pain on one side (mittelschmerz)
- Some people notice increased libido around ovulation

**Important considerations:**
- Cycle tracking alone is not a reliable method of birth control without proper training and consistent use
- Irregular cycles make it harder to predict the fertile window
- Factors like stress, illness, travel, and medications can shift ovulation timing

If you have questions about fertility, whether you are trying to conceive or trying to avoid pregnancy, a healthcare provider can offer personalized guidance.
sourceName: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development

title: Perimenopause and Cycle Changes
body:
Perimenopause is the transition period leading up to menopause, typically beginning in the mid-40s but sometimes starting earlier. During this time, hormone levels fluctuate unpredictably, causing changes in your menstrual cycle.

**Common cycle changes during perimenopause:**
- Cycles may become shorter (under 25 days) or longer (over 38 days)
- Periods may become heavier or lighter than usual
- You may skip periods entirely for one or more months, then have them return
- Spotting between periods may occur
- PMS symptoms may worsen or change in character

**Other symptoms of perimenopause:**
- Hot flashes and night sweats
- Sleep disturbances
- Mood changes, including increased irritability or anxiety
- Vaginal dryness
- Changes in libido

**When to see a healthcare provider:**
- Periods that are extremely heavy (soaking a pad or tampon every hour)
- Bleeding that lasts longer than 7 days
- Spotting between periods or after intercourse
- Periods that occur more frequently than every 21 days
- Any bleeding after you have gone 12 months without a period

Tracking your cycle during perimenopause gives your provider valuable information for managing this transition.
sourceName: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development

title: Menopause: What to Expect
body:
Menopause is a natural biological milestone, not a medical condition. It is officially defined as 12 consecutive months without a menstrual period and typically occurs between ages 45 and 55, with the average age being 51.

**What happens at menopause:**
- The ovaries stop releasing eggs and produce much less estrogen and progesterone
- Menstrual cycles end permanently
- Fertility ends, though pregnancy is still possible during perimenopause

**Common experiences during and after menopause:**
- **Hot flashes:** Sudden feelings of warmth, often in the face and upper body, lasting seconds to minutes. Affect up to 75 percent of people going through menopause
- **Sleep changes:** Night sweats and hormonal shifts can disrupt sleep
- **Vaginal and urinary changes:** Reduced estrogen can cause vaginal dryness, discomfort during intercourse, and increased susceptibility to urinary tract infections
- **Bone density changes:** Estrogen helps protect bones; after menopause, bone loss accelerates, increasing the risk of osteoporosis
- **Mood changes:** Some people experience increased anxiety, irritability, or feelings of sadness

**When to see a healthcare provider:**
- Any bleeding that occurs after 12 months without a period (postmenopausal bleeding must always be evaluated)
- Symptoms that significantly affect your quality of life
- Concerns about bone health or heart health after menopause

Many effective treatments exist for managing menopausal symptoms. Your healthcare provider can help you find the right approach.
